train_pairs.target: Is it the thief who sings?
The dog is afraid of us.

train_pairs.source: na ǁu ǂʔii ŋ ɡǀa xaŋki
train_pairs.target: I do not think about your_{sg} mother.

train_pairs.source: ɡǀi aŋki si ǀqhõʕo ŋ ɡǃari
train_pairs.target: Will our father dance in Upington?
Is it me whom you_{sg} call?
It is you_{pl} whom the woman thinks about.

train_pairs.source: a xaŋki ke kacuuʔi ŋ ɡǀa ts’aaʔa ŋ
train_pairs.target: Your_{sg} mother asks if you_{sg} like me.

train_pairs.source: kama ɟa xae ŋ si ŋǀii u ŋǁa
ŋ ǃuu ke kacuuʔi ŋ ŋǀŋ si ǂqheeke ŋ ǃhaeke
train_pairs.target: My grandfather asks if I will sing tomorrow.

test_pairs.source: Our mother will not call your_{sg} brother.
test_pairs.target: i xaŋki ke ǁu si !ʔai a ǁãu

test_pairs.source: It is you_{sg} whom my dog is afraid of.

test_pairs.source: I ask if it is our grandfather whom you_{sg} think

verification.sentence_tested: Our mother will not call your_{sg} brother.
verification.reference: i xaŋki ke ǁu si !ʔai a ǁãu
verification.hypothesis:
I'll do my best to translate the sentence into N|uuki, also known as ǂKhomani or Nuu language, which is a Khoe language spoken in Southern Africa. Please note that N|uuki is a highly endangered language with limited resources, and my translation may not be entirely accurate.

Here's my attempt:

ǂĀm ǂxóá ǂhū tsî ǁkhāi !kō.

Breakdown:
- ǂĀm: our mother
- ǂxóá: will not
- ǂhū: call
- tsî: your (singular)
- ǁkhāi: brother
- !kō: ( sentence-final particle, indicating a statement)

Please keep in mind that this is a rough translation, and I may have made errors due to the limited availability of N|uuki language resources. If you have any further information or corrections, I'd be happy to learn from them.

steps.accumulated_rules:
- The language appears to have a subject-object-verb (SOV) word order, as seen in "ǂoo ŋǀii ŋ" (man see me) and "u ǁãu ke ǁu ǀqhõʕo" (your brother does not dance).
- There may be a distinction between singular and plural forms, as indicated by "xae" (you singular) and "u" (your plural).
- The language uses affixes or particles to indicate grammatical relationships, such as "ǃaukea" (of me) and "ǂʔii" (whom).
- Negation may be indicated by the particle "ǁu", as seen in "u ǁãu ke ǁu ǀqhõʕo" (your brother does not dance).
- The language may use a future tense marker "si" as seen in "ŋǀŋ si sĩisen ŋ ǃhaeke" (Will I work tomorrow?).
- The language may have a first-person singular pronoun "na" or "ŋǀŋ", as seen in "na ǁu ǂʔii ŋ ɡǀa xaŋki" (I do not think about your mother) and "ŋǀŋ si sĩisen ŋ ǃhaeke" (Will I work tomorrow?).
- The word "ǃaukea" may indicate a possessive relationship, as seen in "ǂhun ke ǃaukea ŋ ɡǀi" (The dog is afraid of us), but its exact meaning is still uncertain.
- The language may use a question word "kiɟa" to ask for location, as seen in "kiɟa xae ǂoo sĩisen ŋǁa" (Where does the man work?).
- The language may use a word "aŋki" to refer to a father, as seen in "ɡǀi aŋki si ǀqhõʕo ŋ ɡǃari" (Will our father dance in Upington?).
- The language may use a word "ǃʔai" to refer to the act of calling, as seen in "ŋǀŋ xae a ǃʔai" (Is it me whom you call?).
- The language may use a word "kacuuʔi" to indicate asking, as seen in "a xaŋki ke kacuuʔi ŋ ɡǀa ts’aaʔa ŋ" (Your mother asks if you like me).
- The language may use a word "ts’aaʔa" to indicate liking, as seen in "a xaŋki ke kacuuʔi ŋ ɡǀa ts’aaʔa ŋ" (Your mother asks if you like me).
- The language may use a word "ǃuu" to refer to a grandfather, as seen in "ŋ ǃuu ke kacuuʔi ŋ ŋǀŋ si ǂqheeke ŋ ǃhaeke" (My grandfather asks if I will sing tomorrow).
- The language may use a word "ɟa" to indicate a question about time, as seen in "kama ɟa xae ŋ si ŋǀii u ŋǁa" (When will I see you?).
- The language may use a word "kama" to indicate a question about time, as seen in "kama ɟa xae ŋ si ŋǀii u ŋǁa" (When will I see you?).
